meta.title: 5.2 Verifying AI Output
meta.description: Learn to check AI-generated web code for structural issues, visual bugs, broken logic, and accessibility problems.

blocks.prompt: You ask AI to build a contact form and it gives you code that looks reasonable at first glance. What kinds of things could be wrong with it that you would not notice without reading the code carefully?

blocks.content:
## AI is fast but careless AI can produce a full webpage in seconds. That is impressive — but speed is not the same as correctness. AI-generated code frequently contains subtle issues that look fine at first glance but cause real problems. The good news: you can now read the code. That means you can verify it. ## The verification checklist When AI gives you web code, run through these checks: ### 1. Is the HTML structure sensible? - Does it use semantic elements? A navigation section should be `
`, not `
`. A clickable action should be `

blocks.question: On a scale of 1-5, how confident are you that you could take a block of AI-generated HTML, CSS, and JS and identify at least two potential issues without running it in a browser?
blocks.answer: If you rated yourself 3 or higher, you are building a strong review instinct. The checklist approach — structure, selectors, logic, responsiveness, accessibility — gives you a systematic way to catch problems before they reach real users.

options: A) The `<div>` should be a `<button>` — keyboard users cannot tab to a div, and screen readers will not announce it as a button
B) The `cursor: pointer` property is unnecessary on a button
C) The `onclick` attribute should be `onClick` with a capital C
problems.explanation: A <div> is not a focusable or interactive element by default. Keyboard users cannot tab to it, screen readers do not announce it as a button, and it does not respond to the Enter or Space keys. The fix is to use <button> instead, which gets all of that behavior for free. This is one of the most common AI mistakes.

options: A) The media query should use `max-width: 768px` instead of `min-width: 768px`
B) The `.hamburger` class name is misspelled
C) The `display: block` should be `display: flex`
problems.explanation: min-width: 768px means "on screens 768px and wider" — that is tablets and desktops. The hamburger menu should show on small screens, so the query should be max-width: 768px ("on screens 768px and narrower"). This is an extremely common AI mistake because the two directions are easy to confuse.

problems.question: AI generated a gallery with these images: ```html ``` The page looks great visually. What is missing?
options: A) Each `` is missing an `alt` attribute, which means screen reader users get no information about what the images show
B) The images need `width` and `height` attributes to load properly
C) The `src` attributes should use absolute URLs instead of relative paths
problems.explanation: The alt attribute provides a text description of the image for screen reader users, and it also displays when the image fails to load. Without alt text, screen readers either skip the image entirely or read the file name ("coffee-shop-dot-jpg"), which is unhelpful. Every image should have a meaningful alt attribute like alt="Interior of a cozy coffee shop with warm lighting".

options: A) The `font-size` is too large for the container
B) The text color and background color are both `#ffffff` (white on white), making the text invisible
C) The `padding` is pushing the text out of view
problems.explanation: Both background-color and color are set to #ffffff, which is white. The text is there — it is just white text on a white background, making it invisible. This is a color contrast failure that also creates an accessibility problem. The fix is to change either the background or the text color so there is sufficient contrast between them.

blocks.prompt: In your own words, explain why using a <button> element matters more than styling a <div> to look like a button. Who is affected, and what do they lose when AI uses a <div> instead?

blocks.content: AI-generated code needs verification. Check five things: HTML structure and semantic elements, CSS selectors matching the HTML, JavaScript working without console errors, the layout at different screen sizes, and accessibility (alt text, real buttons, color contrast, keyboard navigation). The most common AI mistakes are using div instead of button, missing alt text, wrong media query directions, and mismatched selectors.

blocks.prompt: Now you can spot problems in AI output. But spotting a problem is only half the battle — next, you need to tell the AI how to fix it. Module 5.3 will teach you how to describe visual and functional problems in code terms so the AI fixes them on the first try.

reviewQuestions.question: When verifying AI-generated code, the five things to check are:
options: File size, load speed, SEO, analytics, and ads
HTML structure, CSS selectors matching HTML, no JS console errors, responsive layout, and accessibility
Color scheme, fonts, animations, hover effects, and transitions
reviewQuestions.explanation: Check HTML structure, CSS-HTML alignment, JavaScript errors in console, responsive behavior at different sizes, and accessibility (alt text, buttons, contrast).
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's the checklist! Structure, selectors, console, responsive, accessible.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Verify five things: HTML structure, CSS selectors matching HTML, no JS console errors, layout at different screen sizes, and accessibility.

reviewQuestions.question: The most common issue with AI-generated code that looks fine visually is:
options: It uses the wrong programming language
Accessibility problems (missing alt text, divs instead of buttons, poor color contrast)
reviewQuestions.explanation: AI-generated code often looks fine but has accessibility issues — non-semantic elements, missing alt text, and poor color contrast that affects real users.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! AI often misses accessibility — that's where human review matters most.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: AI code frequently has accessibility issues: divs used instead of semantic buttons, missing alt text on images, and insufficient color contrast.
